questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statement: The new AI system can detect roof falls in mines 30 seconds before they occur. Conclusion I: Mine safety will significantly improve. II: Human monitoring will become completely redundant.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some software is free. All free things are valuable. No valuable thing is cheap. Conclusions: I. Some software is not cheap. II. All free software is valuable.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A dice numbered 1-6. If 1 is at top and 2 at front, which is at bottom if standard dice?

questions.stem: From 4 officers and 8 soldiers, a squad of 2 officers and 4 soldiers. A specific officer must be included. Number of squads:

questions.stem: Table: Alloy composition. Metal P = 40%, Q = 30%, R = 20%, S = 10%. In 800 kg, quantity of P exceeds R by:

questions.stem: Five boxes stacked. Box 1 below Box 3. Box 4 above Box 1. Box 2 below Box 5. Box 3 above Box 5. Which is bottommost?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statement: Company X has launched a new product with 30% higher efficiency than competitors. Conclusion I: Company X will capture the market. II: Competitors will also improve efficiency.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All A are B. Some B are C. All C are D. No D is E. Conclusions: I. No A is E. II. Some C are not E.

questions.stem: In a dice, faces are 1 to 6. If 3 is at top, 5 at front, what is at bottom in standard dice?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A transport company must send 8 trucks. 3 are needed for emergency. Only 5 are available. How many combinations possible?

questions.stem: Pie chart of 360 students: Science=40%, Arts=25%, Commerce=20%, Others=15%. Commerce students:

questions.stem: Among 6 friends, P is taller than Q but shorter than R. S is taller than T but shorter than Q. U is shortest. Third tallest:

questions.stem: Statement: Several states have announced drought relief packages. Conclusion I: These states are facing drought. II: Central government will also announce relief.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Only lions are tigers. No tiger is bear. Conclusions: I. No lion is bear. II. All tigers are lions.

questions.stem: An examination has 6 subjects. A student must pass at least 4. How many combinations of passing subjects?

questions.stem: How many straight lines can be drawn through 10 points no three collinear?

questions.stem: In a row of 15, Ramesh is 7th from left. Suresh is 9th from right. How many between them?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a circle of 8, M is opposite N. O is 2nd right of M. P is adjacent to N. Q is between O and M. Who is 2nd left of P?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If 15 March 2026 is Sunday, what day is 15 August 2026?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some red are blue. No blue is green. All green are yellow. Conclusions: I. Some red are not green. II. No blue is yellow.

questions.stem: Find the next: ZA, YB, XC, WD, ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of ARDENT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: The automobile industry is shifting towards electric vehicles. Q: Battery technology has improved significantly in recent years. R: Charging infrastructure is being expanded rapidly. S: This transition will reduce carbon emissions.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of AUDACIOUS:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Climate change is one of the biggest challenges today. Q: Rising temperatures cause extreme weather events. R: Governments worldwide are taking mitigation measures. S: However, collective action at individual level is also needed.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of ABUNDANT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Natural disasters cause widespread destruction. Q: Early warning systems can reduce the impact. R: Preparedness and planning are essential. S: Governments invest in disaster management infrastructure.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of TRANQUIL:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Coal is formed over millions of years. Q: Under heat and pressure, peat transforms into coal. R: Dead plant material accumulates in swamps forming peat. S: This process is called coalification.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of HARMONY:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Democracy is a system of government by the people. Q: Citizens elect their representatives through free and fair elections. R: These representatives make laws on behalf of the people. S: An independent judiciary ensures that laws are followed.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of EPHEMERAL:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: The Green Revolution increased agricultural productivity. Q: High-yield crop varieties were developed and introduced. R: Chemical fertilisers and pesticides were used extensively. S: This helped many countries achieve food security.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of LETHARGIC:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Stock markets reflect the health of an economy. Q: Share prices fluctuate based on supply and demand. R: Investors buy and sell shares of publicly listed companies. S: Market regulators ensure fair trading practices.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of EMANCIPATE:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Artificial intelligence is transforming industries. Q: Machine learning algorithms analyse vast amounts of data. R: AI-powered systems can make predictions and recommendations. S: Ethical considerations around AI are being debated.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of FERVENT:

questions.stem: Meaning of 'A piece of cake':

questions.stem: The evidence ____ against the accused.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of PRAGMATIC:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The committee has ____ the matter thoroughly.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of FERVENT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The document ____ the terms of the agreement.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of BENEVOLENT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of PROCRASTINATE:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SI unit of thermal conductivity is:
questions.solution: Thermal conductivity k is measured in watt per metre per kelvin.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bernoulli's equation is fundamentally a statement of:
options: Conservation of energy along a streamline
questions.solution: It balances pressure, kinetic and potential energy per unit weight along a streamline (no losses).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An Otto-cycle engine has a compression ratio of 8 (γ = 1.4). Its air-standard efficiency is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a constant-volume (isochoric) process the work done by a closed system is:
questions.solution: W = ∫p dV = 0 when volume is constant, so all heat changes internal energy.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 10 cm (V₁ = 2 m/s) to 6 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><path d="M20 30 L150 30 L150 45 L280 45 L280 70 L150 70 L150 85 L20 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₁=10cm</text><text x="210" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₂=6cm</text><line x1="60" y1="57" x2="100" y2="57" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="100,57 93,53 93,61" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="60" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">V₁=2m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Contracting pipe (continuity)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A four-stroke engine completes one power stroke every:
questions.solution: Suction, compression, power and exhaust occupy two crankshaft revolutions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Euler's critical buckling load of a column is proportional to:
options: EI and inversely to the length
EI and inversely to the square of effective length
questions.solution: P_cr = π²EI/L_e²; it depends on flexural rigidity and end conditions, not directly on yield stress.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows from a 30 cm² pipe (velocity 2 m/s) into a 15 cm² pipe. The new velocity is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the effectiveness–NTU method, NTU (number of transfer units) is defined as:
questions.solution: NTU = UA/C_min characterises the heat-exchanger size relative to the minimum heat-capacity rate.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the p–V diagram shown, two expansions start from the same state (V₁ ≈ 1). The steeper curve (1) is the:
questions.solution: Adiabatic curves (pV^γ) are steeper than isothermal curves (pV) on a p–V diagram because γ > 1.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M50 30 C 90 70 140 100 220 112" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M50 45 C 95 85 150 108 220 118" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/><text x="150" y="60" font-size="9" fill="#2563eb">curve 1 (steeper)</text><text x="150" y="100" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">curve 2 (flatter)</text><text x="232" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#475569">V</text><text x="34" y="14" font-size="10" fill="#475569">p</text></svg>
figure.caption: p–V diagram: adiabatic vs isothermal

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Factor of safety is defined as:
questions.solution: FoS = failure stress/allowable working stress; it is always greater than 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a converging subsonic nozzle, as area decreases the velocity ____ and pressure ____:
questions.solution: Continuity speeds up the flow; Bernoulli then drops the pressure as velocity rises.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A spring of stiffness 500 N/m is stretched 0.05 m. The restoring force is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Adding a fin to a surface improves heat dissipation only when the fin's:
questions.solution: A fin helps only if it transfers more heat than the bare base area it covers (effectiveness > 1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a cycle, 1000 kJ of heat is added and 400 kJ rejected per cycle. The thermal efficiency is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The air-standard cycle for a compression-ignition (diesel) engine is the:
questions.solution: CI engines approximate the constant-pressure heat-addition Diesel cycle.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between T₁ (hot) and T₂ (cold), in kelvin, is:
questions.solution: Carnot efficiency η = 1 − T₂/T₁ with temperatures in kelvin.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Carnot engine takes 2000 kJ from a source at 900 K and rejects to 300 K. The work output is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For a circular shaft in torsion, the relation T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L is the:
questions.solution: The torsion equation links torque, polar moment J, shear stress and twist.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A wall (k = 50 W/m·K, area 2 m², thickness 0.2 m) has a 50 K temperature difference. The conduction rate is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On Mohr's circle for plane stress, the principal stresses are located at:
options: The top and bottom of the circle
The points where the circle crosses the normal-stress axis (shear = 0)
questions.solution: Principal stresses occur where shear stress is zero — the circle's intersections with the σ axis.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Clausius inequality ∮ dQ/T ≤ 0 is satisfied with the equality sign only for:
questions.solution: ∮dQ/T = 0 for reversible cycles and < 0 for irreversible ones.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 20 cm (V₁ = 4 m/s) to 5 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><path d="M20 30 L150 30 L150 45 L280 45 L280 70 L150 70 L150 85 L20 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₁=20cm</text><text x="210" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₂=5cm</text><line x1="60" y1="57" x2="100" y2="57" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="100,57 93,53 93,61" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="60" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">V₁=4m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Contracting pipe (continuity)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The coefficient of performance (COP) of a refrigerator is:
questions.solution: COP_ref = Q_absorbed/W_input, and can be greater than 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A solid shaft of 30 mm diameter transmits a torque of 400 N·m. The maximum shear stress is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The main function of a flywheel is to:
options: Reduce fluctuation of speed over a cycle
Control mean speed for load change
questions.solution: A flywheel stores/returns energy to smooth speed fluctuations; a governor controls mean speed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The air-standard cycle for a spark-ignition (petrol) engine is the:
questions.solution: SI engines approximate the constant-volume heat-addition Otto cycle.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 10 kg body moves at 10 m/s. Its kinetic energy is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The COP of a reversed Carnot refrigerator working between T₁ (hot) and T₂ (cold) is:
questions.solution: COP_ref = T_cold/(T_hot − T_cold), with absolute temperatures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the simply supported beam shown (central load W = 20 kN, span L = 6 m), the maximum bending moment is:
questions.solution: For a central point load, M_max = WL/4 = 20×6/4 = 30 kN·m.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><line x1="30" y1="60" x2="270" y2="60" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="3"/><polygon points="30,60 22,78 38,78" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><polygon points="270,60 262,78 278,78" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="150" y1="20" x2="150" y2="58" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="150,58 145,48 155,48" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="150" y="16" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626">W=20kN</text><text x="150" y="96" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">span L=6 m (load at mid-span)</text></svg>
figure.caption: Simply supported beam, central load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For steady incompressible flow in a pipe, the continuity equation gives:
questions.solution: Mass conservation for constant density: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The whirling (critical) speed of a shaft corresponds to its:
questions.solution: At the critical speed the rotational speed matches a natural frequency, causing large deflections.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water (ν = 10⁻⁶ m²/s) flows at 3 m/s in a 0.2 m pipe. The Reynolds number is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a rectangular section (width b, depth d) about its centroidal horizontal axis is:
questions.solution: I = bd³/12 about the centroidal axis parallel to the width.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 1000 mm bar elongates by 0.5 mm under load. Its strain (×10⁻³) is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A sudden change of cross-section in a loaded part causes:
questions.solution: Geometric discontinuities raise local stress well above the nominal value.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Enthalpy h is defined as:
questions.solution: Enthalpy h = u + pv, a convenient property for flow processes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows from a 30 cm² pipe (velocity 4 m/s) into a 5 cm² pipe. The new velocity is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a lathe, the principal cutting operation produces a surface of revolution by:
questions.solution: Turning removes material from a rotating workpiece using a single-point tool.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 20 cm (V₁ = 3 m/s) to 10 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><path d="M20 30 L150 30 L150 45 L280 45 L280 70 L150 70 L150 85 L20 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₁=20cm</text><text x="210" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₂=10cm</text><line x1="60" y1="57" x2="100" y2="57" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="100,57 93,53 93,61" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="60" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">V₁=3m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Contracting pipe (continuity)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The zeroth law of thermodynamics defines the concept of:
questions.solution: If two bodies are each in thermal equilibrium with a third, they are with each other — the basis of temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The gyroscopic couple acting on a rotating disc of moment of inertia I, spin ω and precession Ω is:
questions.solution: Gyroscopic couple C = Iω·Ω, perpendicular to both spin and precession axes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows at 5 m/s through a pipe of area 0.05 m². The volume flow rate is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A material that undergoes large plastic deformation before fracture is described as:
questions.solution: Ductile materials (e.g. mild steel) show significant elongation before failure.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the tensile stress–strain curve shown for mild steel (yield ≈ 250 MPa), point A (end of the initial straight line) represents the:
questions.solution: Point A is the yield point where the linear elastic region ends; the peak (B) is the ultimate strength.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 150" width="280" height="150"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="260" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M30 120 L90 50 Q100 45 115 48 L150 40 Q190 30 210 55 L230 95" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="92" cy="49" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="100" y="44" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">A</text><circle cx="172" cy="33" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="178" y="30" font-size="9" fill="#16a34a">B</text><text x="250" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#475569">strain</text><text x="34" y="14" font-size="10" fill="#475569">stress</text></svg>
figure.caption: Tensile stress–strain curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The velocity ratio of a simple gear train depends on the:
options: Number of teeth on the gears
questions.solution: Speed ratio = N_driver/N_driven = (teeth on driven)/(teeth on driver).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum-shear-stress (Tresca) theory of failure compares the maximum shear stress with:
options: Half the yield stress in simple tension
questions.solution: Tresca predicts yielding when τ_max = σ_yield/2 (the shear at yield in a tensile test).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the cantilever shown (end load W = 5 kN, length L = 2 m), the maximum bending moment (at the fixed end) is:
questions.solution: For a cantilever with an end load, M_max = WL = 5×2 = 10 kN·m at the fixed end.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><rect x="20" y="35" width="12" height="50" fill="#94a3b8"/><line x1="32" y1="60" x2="260" y2="60" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="260" y1="22" x2="260" y2="58" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="260,58 255,48 265,48" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="260" y="18" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626">W=5kN</text><text x="150" y="96" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">cantilever length L=2 m (load at free end)</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cantilever with end load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an isothermal process the property held constant is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water issues from an orifice under a head of 5 m. The ideal jet velocity (Torricelli) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The endurance (fatigue) limit of a material is the stress below which it:
options: Can endure virtually infinite load cycles
questions.solution: Below the endurance limit, ferrous materials survive an effectively unlimited number of cycles.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Newton's law of cooling describes heat transfer by:
questions.solution: q = hAΔT relates convective heat transfer to the surface–fluid temperature difference.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A spring of stiffness 200 N/m is stretched 0.1 m. The restoring force is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Resilience of a material refers to its capacity to absorb energy:
questions.solution: Resilience = strain energy stored up to the elastic limit; toughness is up to fracture.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A driver gear (20 teeth) at 1200 rpm meshes with a 75-tooth gear. The driven gear speed is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Stefan–Boltzmann law states that radiated energy is proportional to:
options: The fourth power of absolute temperature
questions.solution: E = σT⁴ for a black body; radiation rises steeply with temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Balancing of rotating masses is done primarily to:
questions.solution: Unbalanced rotating masses create centrifugal forces causing vibration; balancing cancels them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 5 kg body moves at 4 m/s. Its kinetic energy is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fatigue failure of a component typically occurs at a stress that is:
options: Below the static yield stress, after many cycles
Equal to the yield stress every time
Above the ultimate stress in one cycle
questions.solution: Repeated/fluctuating loads cause crack growth and failure below the static yield strength.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A refrigerator extracts 400 W of heat using 100 W of work. Its COP is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A throttling (Joule–Thomson) process across a valve is characterised by constant:
questions.solution: Adiabatic throttling with negligible KE/PE keeps h₁ = h₂; temperature usually changes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle with compression ratio r is:
questions.solution: η_Otto = 1 − 1/r^(γ−1); efficiency rises with compression ratio.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pin-ended column (E = 200 GPa, I = 4×10⁻⁶ m⁴, length 3 m) has an Euler buckling load of about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A cam-and-follower mechanism is used to obtain a:
options: Prescribed follower motion from a rotating cam
questions.solution: The cam profile dictates the desired displacement–time motion of the follower.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A specimen shows a stress of 100 MPa at a strain of 0.0005. Its Young's modulus is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fourier's law governs heat transfer by:
questions.solution: q = −kA dT/dx is the law of heat conduction.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The bending stress in a beam varies with distance y from the neutral axis as:
questions.solution: Flexure formula σ = My/I; stress is maximum at the outermost fibre.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the p–V diagram shown, two expansions start from the same state (V₁ ≈ 2). The steeper curve (1) is the:
questions.solution: Adiabatic curves (pV^γ) are steeper than isothermal curves (pV) on a p–V diagram because γ > 1.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M50 30 C 90 70 140 100 220 112" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M50 45 C 95 85 150 108 220 118" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/><text x="150" y="60" font-size="9" fill="#2563eb">curve 1 (steeper)</text><text x="150" y="100" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">curve 2 (flatter)</text><text x="232" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#475569">V</text><text x="34" y="14" font-size="10" fill="#475569">p</text></svg>
figure.caption: p–V diagram: adiabatic vs isothermal

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Boundary-layer separation on a body occurs primarily because of:
questions.solution: When pressure rises in the flow direction, near-wall fluid decelerates and reverses, separating the layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The gauge pressure at a depth of 15 m in water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A long, slender column under axial compression is most likely to fail by:
questions.solution: Slender columns fail by elastic buckling (Euler) well before the crushing stress is reached.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ratio of specific heats γ for a gas equals:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A driver gear (25 teeth) at 1200 rpm meshes with a 60-tooth gear. The driven gear speed is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The heat-treatment process used to soften steel and relieve internal stresses is:
questions.solution: Annealing heats and slowly cools steel, softening it and relieving stresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A refrigerator extracts 200 W of heat using 100 W of work. Its COP is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Reynolds number represents the ratio of:
questions.solution: Re = ρVD/μ = inertia/viscous; it indicates laminar vs turbulent flow.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an adiabatic process the quantity that is zero is:
questions.solution: Adiabatic ⇒ Q = 0; the gas may still do work and change temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A wall (k = 200 W/m·K, area 1 m², thickness 0.2 m) has a 100 K temperature difference. The conduction rate is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Slow, continuous deformation of a material under constant load at high temperature is called:
questions.solution: Creep is time-dependent plastic strain that becomes significant at high temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A shaft transmits a torque of 150 N·m at 600 rpm. The power transmitted is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ideal-gas equation of state is:
questions.solution: pV = mRT relates pressure, volume and absolute temperature for an ideal gas.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Poisson's ratio is the ratio of:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Carnot engine works between 600 K and 400 K. Its efficiency is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For any irreversible process in an isolated system, the total entropy:
questions.solution: The second law: entropy of an isolated system increases for irreversible processes (= 0 only if reversible).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the cantilever shown (end load W = 10 kN, length L = 3 m), the maximum bending moment (at the fixed end) is:
questions.solution: For a cantilever with an end load, M_max = WL = 10×3 = 30 kN·m at the fixed end.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><rect x="20" y="35" width="12" height="50" fill="#94a3b8"/><line x1="32" y1="60" x2="260" y2="60" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="260" y1="22" x2="260" y2="58" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="260,58 255,48 265,48" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="260" y="18" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626">W=10kN</text><text x="150" y="96" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">cantilever length L=3 m (load at free end)</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cantilever with end load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Brinell and Rockwell tests are used to measure a material's:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The second law of thermodynamics implies that heat, on its own, flows from:
options: A hotter body to a colder body
A colder body to a hotter body
questions.solution: Spontaneous heat flow is from high to low temperature; the reverse needs work (Clausius statement).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the cantilever shown (end load W = 15 kN, length L = 2 m), the maximum bending moment (at the fixed end) is:
questions.solution: For a cantilever with an end load, M_max = WL = 15×2 = 30 kN·m at the fixed end.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><rect x="20" y="35" width="12" height="50" fill="#94a3b8"/><line x1="32" y1="60" x2="260" y2="60" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="260" y1="22" x2="260" y2="58" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="260,58 255,48 265,48" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="260" y="18" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626">W=15kN</text><text x="150" y="96" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">cantilever length L=2 m (load at free end)</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cantilever with end load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Rankine cycle is the ideal cycle for a:
questions.solution: The Rankine cycle (boiler–turbine–condenser–pump) models vapour power plants.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the tensile stress–strain curve shown for mild steel (yield ≈ 350 MPa), point A (end of the initial straight line) represents the:
questions.solution: Point A is the yield point where the linear elastic region ends; the peak (B) is the ultimate strength.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 150" width="280" height="150"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="260" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M30 120 L90 50 Q100 45 115 48 L150 40 Q190 30 210 55 L230 95" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="92" cy="49" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="100" y="44" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">A</text><circle cx="172" cy="33" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="178" y="30" font-size="9" fill="#16a34a">B</text><text x="250" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#475569">strain</text><text x="34" y="14" font-size="10" fill="#475569">stress</text></svg>
figure.caption: Tensile stress–strain curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Brayton cycle is the ideal cycle for a:
questions.solution: The Brayton (Joule) cycle models gas-turbine plants with constant-pressure heat addition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The first law of thermodynamics is essentially a statement of the conservation of:
questions.solution: ΔU = Q − W; energy is conserved as it changes between heat and work.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An axial load of 10 kN acts on a bar of 400 mm² cross-section. The normal stress is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For a flow with Mach number greater than 1, the flow is described as:
questions.solution: Mach number M = V/a; M > 1 is supersonic, where compressibility effects dominate.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A cantilever (50×100 mm section) of length 1 m carries an end load of 6 kN. The maximum bending stress is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The function of a governor is to:
options: Keep the mean speed nearly constant under varying load
questions.solution: A governor adjusts fuel/working-fluid supply to maintain mean speed as load changes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a heat exchanger, LMTD stands for the log-mean of the:
options: Temperature differences at the two ends
questions.solution: LMTD averages the end temperature differences for the heat-transfer rate Q = UA·LMTD.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water (ν = 10⁻⁶ m²/s) flows at 1 m/s in a 0.1 m pipe. The Reynolds number is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Flow in a circular pipe is generally laminar when the Reynolds number is below about:
questions.solution: Below Re ≈ 2000 pipe flow is laminar; above ~4000 it is turbulent.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the simply supported beam shown (central load W = 30 kN, span L = 6 m), the maximum bending moment is:
questions.solution: For a central point load, M_max = WL/4 = 30×6/4 = 45 kN·m.
